entries.text: ISO 19650 Part 4: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Information Exchange Training Course

This material is for the personal use of a delegate attending a course presented by BSI.No part of the materials may be reproduced, stored electronically, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written consent of BSI.
ISO 19650 Part 4: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Information Exchange Training Course
Be aware of the emergency exits
Please switch mobile phone to silent
Please do not use recording devices

Please observe the following key points:For your personal safety, please be aware of the emergency exits from your classroom and the building.The tutor will inform you of the nearest restrooms.Please do not leave valuable items unattended in the classroom. Keep them with you or make other arrangements for their safekeeping.Please be considerate of other delegates, and avoid distractions from the beeping/flashing of your mobile phone.Please do not use recording devices since they may restrict free discussion.The tutor will inform you of the lunch and break schedule. Please return to class on time.The tutor will inform delegates of any area(s) known to be available for smoking/vaping.If there are any special needs, please confirm these now.

The tutor will explain the outline or flow of the course.This course consists of tutorials, delegate activities and discussions.Your delegate workbook contains all materials related to this course, including slides, activities and reference materials. Sample answers to the activities are also in the back of your workbook. Please only refer to the answers for each activity after completing it, or if you’re really stuck.You will get the most out of this course by being an active participant, asking questions and engaging in discussions and activities. Remember there are no silly questions and please feel free to seek the help of your tutor as needed.
Evaluating frameworks, standards, and codes of conduct

Two short breaks will be taken at suitably convenient times in the morning and afternoon. 45 minutes will be given for a lunch break. Additional breaks may be taken as long as agreed by delegates and tutor, and all learning objectives are met. Course activities will be at the tutor’s discretion, depending on time and delegate needs. Finish times may therefore differ to those advertised.
Upon completion of this course, you will be able to:
Use ISO 19650 Part 4 for information requirements and delivery documentation
Examine information exchanges when control of the facility reverts to the appointing party
Interpret a specific information exchange and national conventions as illustrative examples
Explain the factors influencing the selection of software, schemas, and formats

Course aimTo give those tasked with preparing and receiving information exchanges an understanding of the benefits and criteria and the encapsulation of these ideas in standards and best practice, to enable you, to develop detailed implementation plans.Upon completion of this course, you will be able to: Explain the value of informationExplain the factors influencing the selection of software, schemas, and formatsUnderstand the factors in choosing software, schemas and formatsExamine information exchanges when control of the facility reverts to the appointing partyInterpret a specific information exchange and national conventions as illustrative examples

Understanding ISO 19650 is a vital step in understanding the impact of digitization on the construction sector. It is at the forefront of clients expectations and best practice.Part 4 focusses on the content of each individual information exchange. It builds on the concepts in Part 1 and it complements the processes specified in Part 2 and 3.
The key ideas for collaborative BIM: ISO 19650 Part 1

ISO 19650 Part 1 outlines key concepts and principles for collaborative information management, emphasizing structured and sharable information needs, efficient information capture, and supporting multiple outputs, checks, and workflows for improved accuracy and interoperability.

ISO 19650 Part 1 includes some key concepts and principals for collaborative information management:The need for information requirements and ‘pull’ of informationIncorporation of information capability and capacity in appointment processesUsing information planning and the CDE (Common Data Environment)Information needs form:Structured, because we deal with a huge variety of things, attributes and connectionsSharable, because no one organization owns or controls the whole processThe aim is to capture information just once.One input can then support:Many outputs … and new applications, for greater efficiency and suitabilityMany checks … and automated checking, for greater accuracy and reduced waste and errorsMany workflows … and more sharing, interoperability, for better speed and better confidence
Applying these concepts to project delivery
ISO 19650 Part 2 defines tasks for managing information during project delivery, focusing on key decision points to ensure trustworthy information supports each stage of the project.

ISO 19650 Part 2 applies those concepts and principles to project delivery.It specifies the tasks that participants must complete to establish information management:Appointing partyAppointed partiesLeadOthersIt uses key decision points as the main milestones, often aligned to project delivery ‘stages’, at which trustworthy information is needed, so that subsequent project work (design or construction) can build on the project information model.
Applying these ideas to asset management

ISO 19650 Part 3 extends the principles of collaborative information management to the built assets in use, detailing tasks for participants and using 'trigger events' to update and maintain trustworthy information for reliable decision-making.

ISO 19650 Part 3 applies the same concepts and principles to the built asset in use. It also specifies the tasks that participants must complete to continue information management. Appointing partyAppointed partiesLeadOthersIt uses ‘trigger events’ as key constituents of the life cycle, at which updates are made to maintain the trustworthy information, so that subsequent decisions and events can rely on the asset information model.

ISO 19650 Part 5 introduces security considerations to the processes in Parts 2 and 3, balancing information protection against security risks while maintaining the advantages of collaborative information management.

ISO 19650 Part 5 adds a layer of security-mindedness onto the processes in Parts 2 and 3. It values the possession of the information in the information model against national, commercial and individual security risks, without undermining the benefits of collaborative information management.
Health and safety information management within BIM

ISO 19650 Part 6 focuses on health and safety information management within BIM processes, defining structured collaboration, key participant roles, and its relationship with other standards while maintaining a systematic approach to information structuring.

ISO 19650 Part 6 helps manage health and safety information in BIM. It explains how teams collaborate, the roles of key participants, and how it connects with other standards, ensuring a clear and structured approach to information management.
Activity 1: What is the role of ISO 19650 in my organization?
Let’s understand how you think ISO 19650 and collaborative information management impact the way you manage built environment assets.
Consider how the benefits of collaborative information management apply to your organization.
The activity will be followed by a group discussion.
What is the role of ISO 19650 in my organization?

Activity 1: What is the role of ISO 19650 in my organization? Purpose: To understand more about you, in terms of how you believe ISO 19650 and collaborative information management could or does contribute to the way you commission/deliver/manage built environment assets.Duration:15 minutes work 10 minutes feedbackDirections:Consider how the benefits of collaborative information management apply to your organization.You are also encouraged to list key opportunities and risks from sharing structured data.Elements to think about: Is collaborative information management important to your kind of organization? Why is structured information important to your kind of organization?Where can project/asset information save you money?Can structured information speed up delivery of projects?
What is the role of ISO 19650 in my organization?

Part 4 aims to secure the value of the process and the value in the information model.We will look at the key phrases from the Introduction and from the Scope and how the standard ties together the process within an appointment with the longer term considerations. We will also look in more detail into the CDE process.
The importance of each information exchange
A key idea in information management and ISO 19650 is that information sits alongside other physical deliverables.

A key idea in information management and ISO 19650 is that information sits alongside other physical deliverables. Information can be present in unstructured documentation or as structured information (which can be geometrical and/or alphanumerical information). These two may be valuable in different ways:Unstructured documentationFrozen but evidentialOnce off productionSingle method of checking and sign-off and useStructured informationLinkableRepeatable production processesMultiple methods for checking and sign-off and useISO 19650 Part 4 focusses on:The specification of informationTools for its deliveryAssurance before or after deliveryIt is applicable to BOTH unstructured documentation and structured information.
The detailed process and criteria for decision-making for each individual information exchange to ensure the quality of the project or asset information model
Promote a proportionate and sustainable approach to information exchange where the immediate delivery of information does not limit its future use
Emphasize ‘open’ schemas, data formats and conventions whilst specifying when alternatives may be appropriate
Secure benefits arising from collaborative and interoperable building information modelling (BIM)

ISO 19650 Parts 1, 2 and 3 together are a recipe to allow trust to operate within the framework of project and asset appointments. In particular the rules around the CDE (Common Data Environment) are intended to protect and empower the participants. ISO 19650 Part 4 adds value to the process by ensuring that the information model itself is also protected, and its users are empowered. Later we will see that software, schema and formats should be chosen to support the current and future participants.The scope and introduction make this clear.There are criteria which must apply at every stage or trigger event to protect the published information model, whether the information exchange involves BIM, other structured data, or drawings, schedules and reports. Without these criteria, the use of open standards, or consideration of future usage, the process may lack long-term viability and interoperability.….. the Project Information Model or Asset Information Model is at risk of being devalued.
The full life cycle of information covering both project and asset phases includes:

The information process can be thought about within the conventional stage-by-stage, event-by-event appointment process. The full life cycle of information covering both project and asset phases includes: Need and specifyTender and procureDeliverAssureStorePresentExploit
Contract and evidenceChecking sharing, co-development, backgroundingImport, federation, linking
Decision-making and actionNext stage in project deliveryNext events in asset management
Operational needsSocial sustainabilityEnvironmental sustainabilityEconomic sustainability
In addition, the standard encourages the consideration of all three time-horizons.

It is easiest to think about the short-term, as demand and feedback is almost immediate.Most information exchanges are intended to support decision-making and the initiation of the next stage or events. In many cases the next developer or user of the information may be a different participant.Information exchanges also need to support long-term interests of the appointing party (owner/operator) and of the users and society around the built asset.Part 4 wants to make sure the information can be exploited over the short- medium- and long-term.
Information approved for sharing with other appropriate task and delivery teams or with the appointing party.
Information authorized for use in more detailed design, for construction or for asset management.
Information developed by the originator or task team, not accessible to others.
Journal of information transactions, providing an audit trail of information container development.

This slide summarizes the CDE concept described in ISO 19650-1, where models and document are shared ONLY once checked and approved (A), and treated as published only once authorized and, where appropriate, accepted.Information exists in one of a number of ‘states’, which broadly equate to its degree of development towards a formal deliverableTransitions between one state and the next require checks and reviews to take placeWork In Progress (WIP) information is private to its originating organization/team and has the lowest status codeShared information is viewable, usable but not editable by other teams on the project and its use is limited by its status code. The permitted uses of any information within the CDE and its existence as the latest version are indicated through metadata attached to the filePublished information is the deliverable, usually available at/following the end of the project stage or trigger eventArchive is a journal of changes to the information in the CDE which can be used as an audit trail/history in case of disputeNote, it gives teams the rules for sharing information:By requiring there to be a fixed and predictable file naming conventionBy ensuring that status and version are carefully tracked, without depending on appendages to file namesBy ensuring only the author/originator can overwrite shared information containers
Choices to support current and future participants:software, schema, formats
Information containers areplanned, are finite in number, named in a predictable way and have a definite author
Choices to support its immediate and future use:software, schema, formats
How does a CDE add value?

In summary, a CDE (Common Data Environment) supports collaboration across appointments. It is a different problem to tools such as Document Management Systems (DMS) used within a single organization. The problem is ‘trust’.The rules of a CDE are designed to protect the participants and ensure that they can behave in a trustworthy manner. The first five rules are emphasized in ISO 19650 Part 1. Part 4 addresses the last rule, which requires that choices support both current and future participants. The rules of a CDE are also designed to protect the Information Model so as to ensure the information held is trustworthy. The requirements around information containers and Decision points A and B should already be familiar. The same points around software, schemas and formats help secure the value of the information model. ISO 19650 Part 4 examines the criteria to be applied in making these decisions. The same rule in Part 4 also serves to protect the value of the information.
Clause 6.1 and 6.2: Information exchange decision points A & B
Revision (5.4)Identification of issuesAllocation of issuesImplementing changes
Exchange B (6.2)With appropriate statusWhen the information is fit for approvalLate in a delivery stage or operational eventOnce (hopefully)
Exchange A (6.1)With appropriate statusWhen the information is in a coherent stateEarly in a delivery stage or operational eventOften, when changes have been made

ISO 19650 Part 4 is focused on the consistent criteria to be used at decision points A & B. QA (Quality Assurance) can only be applied in the information development in WIP (work-in-progress). QC (Quality Control) applies at the decision points and during the time the information container is in the ‘SHARED’ state. The time that an information containers spends in the ‘shared’ state is crucial to the quality control activity. A) By the task team (leader) prior to sharing – and when.  Exchange with appropriate status:  When the information is in a coherent state  Early in a delivery stage or operational event  Often, when changes have been madeB) By the appointed parties, lead appointed party and appointing party prior to publishing.   Exchange with appropriate status:When the information is fit for approval and acceptanceLate in a delivery stage or operational eventOnce (hopefully)Revision) Clause 5.4 amplifies the ‘Change actions’ for issues and risks as described in Parts 2 and 3. This is the feedback loop of issues and risks, not information containers. Clause 5.4.1 Focus on gateway A (provider), shared state (reviewers) and gateway B (receiver)Clause 5.4.2 Identify - by (any) information reviewer Clause 5.4.3 Allocate - by information receiver (typically lead appointed party)Clause 5.4.4 Implement change – by information provider
Actor who provides information in an information container
Actor who receives information in an information container
Actor who reviews information and its information container

In order to define the criteria at both decision points A and B, ISO 19650 uses three roles:Information providerInformation receiverInformation reviewerThe information reviewer will be different at decision A (task team leader) and decision B (lead appointed party, other appointed parties).The criteria remains the same, though the lead appointed part at B will be expected to take a wider view of the information quality.
Activity 2: How is information valued in my organization?
Let’s understand how better information management impacts the way you manage built environment assets.
Consider, or discuss, how the valuing information is demonstrated in your organization.
The activity will be followed by a group discussion.
How is information valued in my organization?

Activity 2: How is information valued in my organization? Purpose: To understand more about you in terms of how you believe better information management could contribute to the way you commission/deliver/manage built environment assets.Duration:15 minutes work 10 minutes feedbackDirections:Consider, or discuss, how the valuing information is demonstrated in your organization.Elements to think about: How is the CDE used in your project/asset working?How is quality assurance achieved?How is quality control and checking organized?How are feedback and corrections managed?
How is information valued in my organization?

Choosing and testing the applications is a pre-requisite to effective information exchange. We look at the considerations for choosing applications, schemas and format. We review what ‘open’ and ‘interoperability’ mean.
Clause 5.1.2: Mobilization and testing of applications
2: Using:Open schema and data format standardsProprietary or native data formats Where this does not disadvantage any information receivers' immediate or future needs

1: Support:Import of the schemas and data formats of relevant reference informationExport of the requested schemas and data formatsInteraction with any agreed issue and risk registering management tools

3: Capable Appropriate handling of information exchanges including:Importing into a persistent information containerFederating using applications to create a temporary information resourceLinking as information references or semantic web linking

Mobilizations and testing (ISO 19650-2:2018 Clause 5.5 and ISO 19650-3:2020 Clause 5.5) is when the information process is confirmed.ISO 19650 Part 4 adds some critical questions to the process. 1. Can the tools chosen by appointed parties support the information exchange?Authoring (WIP) Applications need these three capabilities.2. Can open standards be used? Is any alternative fair? The standard recognizes the choice and provides the main criteria. 3. How can reviewers and consumers of the information use the information? Are they capable of the appropriate handling of information exchanges including:Importing into a persistent information containerImport should only be done if the information is going to be developedThis represents a change of responsibilityOr if the import is into a database or model server that respects ownershipFederating using applications to create a temporary (virtual) information resourceThis supports the respect for responsibilitiesNeeds tools with the ability to read all relevant formats Linking as information references or semantic web linkingCross referencing can be done in documents and modelsSemantic web allows anyone to add links between information resourcesInformation resources can be local or remote (CDE) or very remote (libraries)
Collaboration applicationsCDE (Common Data Environment)Federation toolsLinkage toolsReview toolsComment toolsRisk registerIssues register (RFI Requests For Information)
Production applicationsImport/backgrounding/linkage InformationLibrary resourcesIssues (RFI)DevelopmentDesignSpecifyProcure/actionExportDeliveryResponses/RFI (Requests For Information)
Exploitation applicationsDashboardsRisk managementOpportunity managementAnalysisBuildabilityOperability(Not covered by ISO 19650 Part 4)

There are two kinds of applications needed for collaborative information management. There are production (WIP) applications. These need to make the best possible use of background information shared by others support, information development, and support the delivery of that information and any comments into the team, typically as RFIs – Requests For Information.And there are collaboration tools…These include CDE applications, viewers, and processes around the comments register.Since only ‘comments’ flow back to the author/originators, these are important. Of course information needs to be put to use, and there are a variety of applications that make the information actionable. This is not in scope for ISO 19650 Part 4.
Interoperability is the ability of information to exist outside of the applications that produce and consume it.

Interoperability has been defined as the ability of two applications to communicate. However, given the large number of participants and the long-time scales associated to built assets, it is more relevant to say that ‘Interoperability is the ability of information to exist outside of the applications that produce it, and outside of the applications that consume it’. Any information exchange will use an underlying schema. A schema defines what kind of information can be represented and how it links together internally. Even a simple document such as an image has a schema that may define what metadata is catered for, and how much information is held about each pixel.Several questions arise about the choice of schema:Unstructured? Are there any restrictions on unstructured documentation? Semi-Structured? Will these remain accessible? Proprietary schemas? Will the information remain accessible? API (Application Programming Interface)Open schemas? Are these suitable for the short, medium and long-term needs? (Industry Foundation Classes (IFC), Geography Markup language (GML)).ISO 19650 Part 4 has Annex A.1 which is informative, suggesting:A.1: Open schema including Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) and regional/sector schemes such as COBie (Construction-Operations Building information exchange), OSCRE (Open Standards Consortium for Real Estate), CFIHOS (Capital Facilities Information Handover Specification), CAFMconnect (Computer-Aided Facility Management connect).
‘Open’ means an accessible schema and format(s).

Any information exchange will also use format. A format (‘serialization’) defines how the information can be stored (on disk) or shared (electronically).  Some schemas have several formats, for example Excel (one proprietary, one open).IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) is usually seen as a STEP Physical file, but can also be serialized as ifcXML, ifcOWL and ifcJSON. Similar questions arise about the choice of format:Unstructured? Are there any restrictions on unstructured documentation? Semi-Structured? Will these remain accessible? Proprietary formats? Will the information remain accessible?Open formats? Are these suitable for the short, medium and long-term needs?The definition of Open usually includes being publicly or easily availabledeveloped by consortia or consensusISO 19650 Part 4 has Annex A.2 which is informative, suggesting:A.2: Open formats including .ifc (SPPF) and .xlsx (open office) and .icdd for information container data drops.
Activity 3: What applications, schemas, formats are in use for my built assets?
Consider, or discuss what applications, schemas and formats are in use, by making a list of these in the table provided.
Consider the opportunities and risks from using these applications, schemas and formats.

Activity 3: What applications, schemas, formats are in use for my built assets? Purpose: To explore what applications, schemas and formats are in use.Duration:15 minutes work 10 minutes feedbackDirections:Consider, or discuss what applications, schemas and formats are in use, by making a list of these in the table provided. You are also encouraged to consider the opportunities and risks from using these applications, schemas and formats.Elements to think about: What applications are in use (for development, and collaboration)?What schemas are supported?What formats are in use?How much commonality is there in the room?How different was this five or ten years ago?
We will look at where information criteria come from, and if there are any exceptions to the review process. We will then look at 3 of the 6 information criteria detailed in ISO 19650 Part 4.
IDM/MVD/IDSBIM specificationsLevel of information need

Where do the criteria for checking information exchanges come from?Supply side: Project/Asset Information DocumentsAIR (Asset Information Requirements)PIR (Project Information Requirements)IS (Information Standard)IPMP (Information Production Methods and Procedures)AppointmentRequirementsEIR (Exchange Information Requirements)Supply sideDuties of careESG (Environmental, social and governance intentions)AppointmentScope of servicesBEP (BIM Execution Plan)Common interestA single source of truthNational annex buildingSMART Information Delivery Manual/Model View Definition/Information Delivery SpecificationISO 12911 BIM specificationsISO 7817-1 Level of information need concepts and principles
Clause 6.4: Possible exceptions to the revision process?
CDE processCDE metadata and naming

Are there any information exceptions to the full CDE (Common Data Environment) process? In particular are there information containers that are not subject to revision? This may apply to information containers that are immutable (unchangeable). Some information containers may be generated automatically by processes and tools. They still need to go through the CDE process with appropriate meta-data and naming, but they can’t be revised, corrected or have their shared status code changed.Some examples are suggested. RFID is a radio-frequency identifier (smart tag) which may be attached to assets. IoT refers to data from Internet-of-Things sensors.
Clause 7 identifies 6 broad areas for information quality:
Informative annex:Open schema and data format standards
IDM/MVD/IDSBIM specificationsLevel of information need

Clause 7 identifies 6 broad areas for information quality:CDE (Common Data Environment): Compliance to the agreed process Conformance: Adherence to schemaContinuity: Avoiding spurious changes of information  Communication: Avoid poor interpretation of dates, characters sets, unitsConsistency: Avoiding gaps, clashes, contradictions, inappropriate accuracy Completeness: Against EIR (Exchange Information Requirements), immediate needs, next stage/event needs and long-term needsIt does mention, but not define, other criteria such as structural or functional.The informative annex offers lists of open schemas and formats. Any national annex will give details of local implementations.For most of the criteria the following cautions apply:An information reviewer shall consider:Status code (perfection may not be achieve initially)The intended code prior to the shared state The assigned code prior to the published stateUsing a formalized procedure when checking against the information requirements or checklists derived from them, possibly including structured rules using repeatable automated processesMeans of sharing checking procedures with information providers through the information production methods and procedures
Check that the requirements of the CDE process are correctly applied.

Clause 7 contains 6 distinct criteria for checking the information quality, and a seventh that mentions ‘real’ requirements such as functionality and so on.Clause 7.1 covers the proper use of the CDE (Common Data Environment). Prior to sharing these requirements may be planned but are realized as part of the sharing process.  The naming of the information container is defined either in the Information Standard or National annex. It should control identifiers for:The originator: This confirms who has the rights to overwrite, who should be informed of issues The project/asset: This may include sub-projects such as phased developmentThe functional content: Typically a system, such as fabric, structure, mechanical, electrical, plumbing and so onThe spatial content: Typically a volume, perhaps a storey (floor) or regionAll information containers are defined from the allowed values for these. There may be other ‘fields’ in the name, such as:e.  Disciplinef.   Form (text, table, model)g.  Unique numberf.   File extension, which may indicate the schema and format Metadata is separate from the name and covers:Status or suitabilityVersion (which may be maintained automatically by the CDE)Classification of the information container (not the contents)Within the CDE, The visibility of an information container or its contents may be restricted to specific groups or individuals. This can be through folders, or other features of the CDE application.
Check against the chosen exchange schema and data format:

Clause 7.2 covers Conformance. Conformance demands that the information container ‘conforms’ to both the schema and format. Schema and format should have been specified, for example in the information standard or recorded in the BIM execution plan.The schema specifies the internal structure, and the format indicates what tools can be used. For example STEP physical format files can be read by all IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) viewers. Some viewers can read ifcXML format as well, but so can any XML tool.Structured information can be tested using a variety of tools.Unstructured documentation and proprietary files can only be tested by using the appropriate applications.
Check the continuity of the content compared with other exchanges:
Attribute, property, material, shape/profile naming and visibility

Clause 7.3 covers Continuity. Continuity means there shouldn't be any (unjustified) change to the common basis for the information model. Of course, these things can change, if the change reflects real need. But spurious change disrupts the common basis. Continuity of the naming of the information container is covered in Clause 7.1 CDE (Common Data Environment).This includes ‘physical’ and ‘temporal’ aspects such as grids and origins, but also the ‘semantic’ aspects such as the naming of the entities.Some of these may be the subject of external standards: Location may be the subject of mapping and sector conventionsDates and week numbers are standardised through ISOThere are some ISO standards affecting naming, for example building storeysProperties are standardised in the buildingSMART data dictionary (bsDD) and elsewhereSome sectors (such as steel) have standardized names of materials and profiles Continuity applies through time but also simultaneously across information containers: Other information containersPrevious versions of the current information containerPrevious status changesPrevious delivery stages and operational trigger eventsPrevious delivery or operational phases
Activity 4: How is the CDE, conformity and continuity managed?
To understand more about the CDE, conformity and continuity management in your organization.
Consider how the CDE, conformity and continuity is managed.
You are also encouraged to list, in the table provided, key opportunities and risks from sharing structured data.

Activity 4: How is the CDE, conformity and continuity managed?Purpose: To understand more about the CDE, conformity and continuity management in your organization.Duration:15 minutes work 10 minutes feedbackDirections:Consider how the CDE, conformity and continuity is managed.You are also encouraged to list, in the table provided, key opportunities and risks from sharing structured data.Elements to think about: Is the CDE properly executed?Is there any interest in structured information, or does unstructured documentation predominate?What kinds of breaks in continuity have affected outcomes? Were these avoidable?

We look at three more criteria, including the most challenging – ‘completeness’ and the methods for specifying and testing. We also consider the criteria not based on information requirements but on ‘real’ requirements.
Check the exchanged information can be or has been stored without degradation or loss due to translation or conversion:

Clause 7.4 covers some common pit-falls relating to the communication of electronic information. Any of these issues can lead to confusion. Confusion can arise if these technical issues are not managed. For example, names, with strange characters can disrupt information exchanges. This needs to be checked during mobilization, and watched for during each information exchange. This cannot be completely checked at decision point A but is critical at decision point B.
Check for consistency with any referenced information containers in terms of:

Clause 7.5 demands the consistency or ‘coherence’. Many practitioners focus first on ‘clash detection/avoidance’ but consistency is far more wide-ranging. Geometric consistency includes eliminating ‘clashes’. Duplication is an extreme kind of ‘clash’. But there is also a need to verify that there are no gaps, particularly in the envelope, in the structure and the distribution systems. It is also possible to discuss the same issues for attribute consistency. Whilst duplication of the same value on an object may be tolerable, differences are unacceptable. Omissions of attributes may also be unacceptable. Numeric accuracy (number of significant figures) and text fields matching expected patterns should be applied consistently.Lastly there are risks arising from the federation/disaggregation:a. Gaps or overlaps with adjacent information containers within the federation strategyb. Incorrect allocation with other information containers within the federation strategy
Check the presence and adequacy of the information against the level of information need and any additional expectations:
Oversight and coordination for the current phase

Clause 7.6: Completeness should be defined in the information standards applicable to the project delivery stage or operation trigger event. In particular the relevant EIR (Exchange Information Requirements) should be referenced. Consideration should include the short- medium- and long-term purposes.This can include: Entities: What objects should be present, including groupings such as zones and systems and packagesRelationships: Any specific relationships (such as the link between spaces and bounding walls, slabs, windows, doors) or between linear elements (such as distribution, and paths, routes, tracks and lanes)Properties: What attributes are required on which types of entityShape: 3D and/or 2D: Level of geometryQuantities: Which can be derived from 3D, which are documented manually?Descriptions: How much explanation is needed? External references. What Documentation? Classification? Library links?
How can completeness be specified and tested?
Spreadsheets/tablesBest driven by purpose principles, not entities
ISO 12911How to write human-readable, well-structured rules
Building SMART approachesIDM: Process mapping to identify information flowsMVD and mvdXML: Machine checkable rulesIDS and idsXML: Simplified checkable rules
ISO 7817Part 1: Entities, properties and documentationPart 2: Specifications

Specifying and testing ‘completeness’ is a challenge…Many EIRs (Exchange Information Requirements) use extensive tables or spreadsheets. These can take huge amounts of effort to maintain the content and the presentation. Many are developed without planning and contain massive duplication/repetition. It will be better to ‘factor’ the information into object groups typically by ‘system’ and property groups by ‘purpose’ and then indicate how they relate. Informal spreadsheets are not easily turned into checks and so may neglected.ISO 12911 encourages writing rule-based specifications that (will) have a direct conversion to checkable rules. Any set of Requirements (need U-value, thermal mass) can be associated to the Selection of objects (Ifc wall, ifc slab) they apply to. Any Exceptions can be noted (outbuildings). Any selected entity can be qualified with a list of narrowing Applicabilities (Ifc Wall, IsExternal).BuildingSMART has developed a process for developing requirements. ISO 29481-1 and ISO 29481-3. The IDM (Information Delivery Manual) methodology starts by mapping information and feedback flows. Each exchange can then be examined, and the information content determined. This can then be formalized as either as an MVD (Model View Definition), (what subset of IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) is needed and any other RASE rules) or a simple IDS (Information Delivery Specification) can be used to express simple rules around applicability and requirements. Selection and exceptions are harder to represent. ISO 7817-1:2024 is the standard covering the concepts and principles and other parts should, in the future, give a structure.
Check for satisfaction of the asset delivery and/or operational requirements:

Clause 7.7 is informative not normative. It serves to remind that information quality sits alongside the ‘real’ qualities of the built asset. Having good information is intended to support decisions about the ‘real’ quality of the built asset.
Activity 5: How is the communication, consistency and completeness managed?
Think about how communication, consistency and completeness management in your organization.

Activity 5: How is the communication, consistency and completeness managed?Purpose: To understand more about the communication, consistency and completeness management in your organization.Duration:15 minutes work 10 minutes feedbackDirections:Consider, and write down in the table provided, how the communication, consistency and completeness is managed.Elements to think about: Have you ever experienced (electronic) communication failure?Are there any consistency checks in place beyond clash detection? What defines completeness of any example documents, drawings or models?

This session we shall firstly look at example information exchange at handover represented as Construction Operations Building Information Exchange or more commonly referred to as COBie. This handover can typically be when control of the facility reverts to the appointing party.This is of importance both in delivery projects and for starting the in-use phase successfully. Some of this section may be specific to a region or country as we shall look at some examples of implementation and use the UK annex within ISO 19650 Part 4 to draw from, which are specific to the UK but can or may be used in other countries where they may not be a national annex or guidance specified. This is of high important to ask the question of what national annex you are working or guidance or specification in order to exchange information successfully.
AssetThe overall facility and:Space (location)Floor (region)ZoneComponent Type System

InformationCollated anyway for:ReviewInspectionSorting and filteringCheckingObtaining from BIM and other sourcesLoading into Facilities Management (FM), Asset Management (AM) and Project Management (PM) tools

We can consider a specific case to apply these ideas about information exchanges and information quality.Example Project delivery stage/operational trigger event: HandoverPurpose: Operations and maintenanceManageable equipment and visitable spaces do not make up the whole facility, but we need floors, zones, systems and types to organize and manage some of them.Every manageable component is associated to a visitable space. Once you know the manageable components, you know the systems and types that need to be documented. If you know the visitable spaces, you know the zones and floors that need to be documented.The information expected may well be available anyway from various sources. Much of it will be required for loading into specific Facility Management (FM), Asset Management (AM) and Portfolio/Capacity Management (PM) applications.
Product data, as-built layout, tag & serial no., warranties & spares
Where is COBie required?Designer deliverableConstruction handover specificationConstruction commissioning specs
Capture data as you goDesign 	ConstructionCommissioning

Easy to useSpreadsheet (other options such as IFC or XML are possible)Each party adds only their partInteroperable with IFC (everything in COBie can be expressed in IFC. Some of IFC is relevant to COBie)

COBie is a data schema to deliver structured asset data in a neutral format. It was developed to help the project client and/or building owner, to receive information about the facility in a format that can be used openly. Construction Operations Building information exchangeFrom construction - it serves the construction industryInto operation - it serves the clientOf building - its scope is a single facility (building or infrastructure section)Information exchange - it supports a repeatable, testable process using dataWhere is COBie required? It is intended to be an evolving deliverable, and/or to be derived from evolving BIM models.Designer/engineer deliverable – including at concept stage and at design completionConstruction handover specification – including contractors proposals and final handoverConstruction commissioning specs – including pre and post handoverCOBie is not:An applicationA design or engineering toolA Facilities, Asset or Portfolio Management (FM/AM/PM) toolA complete representation nor a digital-twin of: A facilityBIM, PIM, AIM

The principle is very simple: What assets are being handed over, what do we need to know about them and in particular how do we operate  and use them?COBie is defined by the US NIBS (National Institute of Building Science NBIMS (National BIM Standard) v3. This document includes the specification of the spreadsheet and of COBie’s close relationship to IFC. This chart has the best overview of the content of COBie. COBie consists of 18 sheets, plus an introductory sheet (Instruction) and a sheet of allowed terms (PickLists) at the end.The larger rectangles start to divide up responsibility:Design and engineering responsibilityBuild and commissioning responsibility, including completion of the ‘Type’ details with product and warranty details, and completion of the ‘component’ details with serial, RFID or batch numbersCommon responsibility for additional supporting information for the spatial and physical assetsEach smaller rectangle indicates one aspect – for example a sheet in the spreadsheet representation, or entity in a database or BIM representation.The arrows indicate some of the main connections between these entities.

This simple building has been marked up to show how existing information is tabulated in COBie. The categories in this example are US OmniclassEach space is associated to a named FloorHere description is expected. Room numbers are rarely enoughRequirable information include areas, and any additional unique names often added late in the construction/commissioning processColours are for human guidance – they are not required by sending or receiving applications.Yellow: Required dataSalmon/pink: Required data keyed from other sheets or the PickListsPurple: Used by the generating application to provide provenance keys. Required if linked with applicationsGreen: Fields that the client appointment may specify as required, giving flexibility. Ideally see the EIR

This floor plan shows the actual spaces and their identifiers that feed directly into COBie worksheets. Each red-boxed label on the drawing - like Locomotive Shop (100A), Recessed Maintenance Area (100B) becomes one row in your COBie output table..For each space you capture in COBie, you’ll populate columns such as:Name e.g. “110A”Description e.g. “Locomotive Shop”Category as defined in your classification (e.g. “Maintenance Spaces”)ExtObject e.g. “IfcSpace”FloorName ← as per your floor breakdown strategyBy mapping every red-outlined area on this plan into COBie, you ensure that the “as-built” spatial structure is delivered in a neutral, machine-readable format—ready for import into Facilty Management, Asset Management or Project Management systems.
COBie’s relationships with BIM and CAFM
BIM can be exported to COBieAutodesk Revit add on Solibri, XBIM, Navisworks, AEC3
COBie can be imported into CAFMImport functionsImport via IFC
COBie can be imported into BIMTransform of COBie to IFCSpreadsheet add-ons
CAFM can be exported to COBieExport functionsExport to IFC

COBie is a simple sub-set of BIM focused on the FM task.A basic low-level feature of COBie is that it can be read and written using:Transcription, though risk of error is highCut-and-paste of whole sheets or selected columns and selected rowsAutomation makes for more repeatable, checkable, improvable processes: BIM can be exported to COBie.Selective filtering is vital as most Components and Attributes are irrelevant. Autodesk Revit add on Via IFC for example Solibri, XBIM, Navisworks, AEC3COBie can be exported to BIM:Transform of COBie2 to IFC for exampleSpreadsheet add-ons to BIM authoring tools COBie can be imported into CAFM (Computer Aided Facility Management, asset management, portfolio management):Import functions for example IBM MaximoImport via IFC for example IBM MaximoCAFM can be exported and filtered to COBie.Filtering is important as COBie does not track task executionExport functions (some US applications)Export to IFC (check with vendors)
COBie is owned by lead appointed party
Trial import into CAFM at mobilisation

Criteria applied to COBieCDE (Common Data Environment)No specific criteriaConformanceSchema: subset of IFCFormat: spreadsheet templateContinuityAllows for changes in the name of spacesCommunicationPreference for [A-Za-z0-9:-_] and spacesConsistencyNo duplication between attributes, impacts, documents and main sheetsClassification on all entriesCompletenessNamed facility, site and project (stage)Named, manageable equipment, their types and their systemsNamed, visitable spaces/locations, their floors/regions and their zonesOther criteriaUseful for operational/Facility ManagementUseful for asset and maintenance/replacement managementUseful for Portfolio Management

We shall no look  at some more implementation details.The majority of this section may be specific to a region or country. An example implementation will need to consider the processes in Part 2 and Part 3.ISO 19650 Part 4 adds the challenge of developing:QA: ‘quality assurance’ – methods which guarantee criteria are met ‘in advance’ by using appropriate applications or restricting or training around some freedoms within toolsQC: ‘quality control’ processes that retrospectively check the criteria ‘in arrears’ with the chance that corrections and rework will be neededQA vs QC:QA is proactive, QC is reactiveQA is about configuration, QC is about outcomesQA is systematic, QC is additionalQA is embedded, QC may be independentSome criteria may be easier/faster/cheaper to quality assure, others may be easier/faster/cheaper to quality control.
Documenting any overriding of this NA and the selection of any options within this NA
Referenced in the project's (ISO 19650-2 5.1.4) or asset's (ISO 19650-3 5.1.6) information standard
Relationship to the UK BIM Framework
Assist in understanding the UK implementation of this standard
A National Annex is to clarify its implementation within a country

ISO 19605 Part 4 Uses ‘shall’ for absolute requirementsUses ’shall consider’ for absolute requirements where the implementation should be proportionate to the project/asset size, type and contextUK National AnnexUses ‘should’ to convey that in the UK context, these are recommendations but become mandatory if cited in a contract or incorporated into a contractual document such as the Project Information Standard. NA.1 anticipates that appointing party documentation may overridePreserves UK practice from  ISO 19650 Part 4 and makes them applicable to ALL information exchangesRefers to supporting material at UK BIM Framework: https://www.ukbimframework.org/buildingSMART UKI resources: https://www.ukbimalliance.org/buildingsmart-uk-ireland-resources/
NA.2.1 ClassificationInformation containerInformation content

NA.2 mandates Uniclass for classifying: Information containers externally using metadataA wide range of information exchange content internally It is important to understand the difference.Note that the classification or risks (and opportunities) using the PM (Project Management) table is up for review.There is some flexibility allowed:Note [2] Uniclass Co (Complexes) can also be used for mixed use buildings, outside facilities and infrastructureNote [3] Uniclass Ss (Systems) can also be used for some systems (EF Elements and Functions) and for assembled Products (Pr)The Uniclass Ss ‘Systems’ table is primarily about Assemblies, the ‘Elements and Functions’ table is primarily about Systems (!)In linear infrastructure chainage can serve a general locational and/or a measured positional role so a note allows for the general locational role.Note [5] Chainage Groups can be analogous to Zones, Chainage can be analogous to Locations.
NA.2.2 Life cycle stages and other classificationsNA.2.3 GeolocationNA.2.5 UnitsNA.2.6 Whole project/asset exchangesZonesSystemsPackages
Stage 0Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3Stage 4 Stage 5 Stage 6 Stage 7
StrategyPreparation and BriefConceptDefinitionTechnical DesignManufacture, Construct and CommissionHandover and ClosureUse

Stage 0: StrategyStage 1: Preparation and BriefStage 2: ConceptStage 3: DefinitionStage 4: Technical DesignStage 5: Manufacture, Construct and CommissionStage 6: Handover and ClosureStage 7: Use
NRM 3_5.1.1.1.3 Wash basins: PlumbingCESMM4 Z-5-3: Sanitary appliances and fittings

NA.2 summarizes the Stage/Phase naming found in BS 8536 which is part of the UK BIM framework. Note [1] Project and life cycle stages are named and defined in BS 8536:2022. See Table 2. IFC and COBie use project or facility ‘Phase’.It also includes mention of some entities for which Uniclass does not provide a classification table. In some cases the alternatives mentioned may be relevant.  Note [4] Jobs, Packages, Events, and Tasks should have breakdown structure assigned as described within the project’s or asset’s information standard, such as, for life cycle costs and carbon (NRM, CESSM 4 and ICMS-3) and for maintenance tasks (SFG20).          Geolocation to OS grid is recommended, but many infrastructure projects will override to use more sophisticated geo-location. Units are expected to be SI and derivatives.The last topic emphasizes that if any information exchange is intended to cover the entire site, entire facility or entire process, then zones, systems and packages should ‘span’ (cover) the entirety. So a complete description of a facility should include a set of systems representing every aspect. Some of the systems may be high-level, some may be more specific.
Role of COBie in UK BIM FrameworkHandover US National BIM Standard V3buildingSMART UKIClause 7  Other developmentsBuilding Equipment MVDCOBie 3 / AIMieFIREie
L : List for .xml (SpreadsheetML)M : Model for .ifc, .ifcXML (IFC)
Form letter and information container classification
Character set is limited to [A-Za-z0-9:-_] and spaces
Recommended UK property sets and properties

Clause NA.3 covers the UK use of COBie for Handover. It emphasises the basis in the US NBIMS v3 document. Additional resources are available at buildingSMART UKI. Some implementations related to Clause 7. Conformance: COBie expects a language code, but IFC a country code. There are some development similar to COBie in process, which may become significant. buildingSMART International are developing a ‘Building Equipment’ MVD ie a subset of IFC. This may be equivalent to COBie for a very narrow purposeUS NIBS is developing COBie3 which will add tabs/tables for packages and for events/tasks. This will allow handover of FM information during the in-use phase because responsibilities and the completed and planned jobs will be knownFIREie has been proposed in BS 8644. This is intended for use at several gateways in the life cycle and includes specific differences from the COBie standard so as to convey fire safety information, not all of which relates to equipment
UniclassUniclass is the preferred classification tablesSystems ‘span’ the complete physical facilityZones ‘span’ the complete spatial facilityPackages  ‘span’ the complete management

COBie4All Provides guidance for infrastructure:Floors interpreted as regions of the siteSpaces interpreted as locations within regionsUse of linear referencingTransmission of lists and tables of data

The UK NA does not change the COBie standard at all except:Uniclass is the preferred classification tablesUK templates are available. These have region: en-UK on the first sheetSystems need not have any manageable ComponentsZones need not have any visitable Spaces  This ensures compatibility with IFC especially during design development and costing     Guidance is provided on use with infrastructure at buildingSMART UKI resources:Floors interpreted as regions of the siteSpaces interpreted as locations within regionsThis interpretation reflects common infrastructure practice, though the spatial hierarchy is sometimes not well understood.

Securing value in processes and information models

1. Understanding ISO 19650Explains the impact of digitization in construction.Highlights Part 4’s focus on individual information exchanges.Builds on concepts from Part 1 and complements processes in Parts 2 and 3.2. Securing Value in Processes and Information ModelsExamines key phrases from the Introduction and Scope.Demonstrates how standards integrate processes into long-term considerations.Explores the Common Data Environment (CDE) process in greater detail.3. Planning, Tools, and FormatsFocuses on choosing and testing applications, schemas, and formats.Reviews the importance of ‘open’ data and ‘interoperability’ for effective exchange.4. Information Criteria and ExceptionsInvestigates sources, exceptions, and key criteria (e.g., CDE, conformance, continuity).Explores 3 of the 6 information criteria from ISO 19650 Part 4.5. Advanced Information CriteriaDiscusses communication, consistency, and completeness.Explains challenges with specifying and testing ‘completeness.’Considers real requirements beyond just information needs.6. Implementation DetailsFocuses on examples of implementing ISO 19650 Part 4 (UK National Annex).Acknowledges regional variations for implementation processes.
Take a moment to consider which topics you are most familiar with and identify the areas in which you wish to focus further reflective learning on.

You should now be able to: Explain the value of informationUse ISO 19650 Part 4 for information requirements and delivery documentationUnderstand the factors in choosing software, schemas and formatsExamine information exchangesUnderstand a specific information exchange and national conventions as examples
